## [00:00 - 03:02]

Quick reminder about the big three. So what we're going to talk about is what the big three are, why they're critical, and really how to eliminate issues with our sales team before it happens. So the context of this is when I was training my account managers and my coaches and so on and so forth, what we discovered is that whenever a sales team isn't performing at the level they need to be, I tell them to always to assess the big three. And the big three is that 95% of the time, when a sales team is not performing, the big three are either not being done at all, not being done correctly, or they're being done correctly but not used to correctly make the right coaching adjustments. So say that again, 95% of the time when a sales team is not performing, the big three, which we're going to cover what those are in a second, are either not being done, not being done correctly, or just not being used to make the right coaching adjustments. So these are critical to understand. I hammer people on these. The big three is proper tracking. We've already been over that in a previous video. Daily meetings, we've already been over that. And then the qualitative end of the day reporting, we've already been over that. So I'm not going to go through these on this training. I just want to remind you here why these are so important. Like if we can get you to do these three things, you're at least like 50% of the way there, if not more. Now why are these important? Because as we covered, you should know this by now, it allows us to create accurate inputs, information of what's really going on with our team. If we don't know what's going on, then we can't coach and lead them effectively. If we're not hitting our numbers, we need the attribution of why we're not hitting our numbers. And the more inputs we have, both qualitative and quantitative, that's what these are, the easier leading the team becomes. So once we have the problem crystal clear, the answer becomes relatively obvious. The problem is a lack of understanding of what the problem really is. So if you come to me and you say, hey, my sales team's not working, dude. Hey, my sales team's underperforming. Well, the first question I always ask, and it kind of sounds pompous a little bit, but the first question I always ask is why are they not performing? And most times, they don't know why. Because if they did know why, if they did have proper attribution, then it's pretty easy to fix it. It's usually pretty obvious. So the real problem is always a lack of understanding of what the actual problem really is. That actually goes for almost all business problems. Usually people aren't stuck because they know what the problem is. They don't know how to fix it. They're stuck because of a lack of attribution of what the real problem is. And so a misdiagnosis on the problem, it's hard to come up with a solution of a problem that is not even your right problem. So anyways, the easier it is to understand the pursuit of clarity of what's happening and visibility of what's happening on the front lines and the real reason we're not hitting our numbers, that's what usually I'm helping people with when their sales teams are stuck. Now, the reason I'm teaching this stuff is because this is how you're going to get the inputs and the data you need to unveil that

## [03:02 - 06:03]

and make those decisions. So here are some of the biggest mistakes here. Now, in terms of tracking, if you don't use my tracking sheet, or at least the same metrics I use, I don't care if you use the sheet, but at least you've got to use the same metrics. If you're not using that, you're setting yourself up to fail. Or if you're not tracking at all. I can't tell you how many times somebody's sales team is not performing and I ask for the tracking sheet and they don't have one. Or they have it, but the stages are all messed up to where all we know is that it's just not working. We're not breaking it down. So we want to break it down this way. So you should know all of these numbers. OK? All of these numbers. And this is what's already in the tracking sheet. I already gave you guys. OK? And then in addition, we should know that our ad spend, our front-end cash ROAS, our revenue ROAS, our total cash collected ROAS. So ad spend is obvious. Front-end cash ROAS is out of the ad spend you got, what's the return on ad spend of the cash you collected upfront? So let's say your program's 10K and on average, you 2Pay, which means you split it into two payments of five, and you pay 1K to acquire a customer. So that's a 5X front-end cash ROAS. But then that would be a 10X revenue ROAS. Does it make sense? So again, if on average, you collect 50% upfront, and your program's 10K, which means you collect on average 5K upfront, and you spend 1K to acquire a customer on average, you have a 5X front-end cash ROAS and a 10X revenue ROAS. And then your total cash collected ROAS is just out of all the ad spend that you spent that month versus all the cash that came into the business, what's the return? So this should be higher than your front-end cash ROAS, obviously. Because this is going to take into account back-end payments, back-end clients, receivables, et cetera. I'd also always know your P&L, and especially know your percentage of expenses when it comes to labor marketing and overhead. If I know all of these numbers in your business, I can literally fix your business in like 10 minutes. But if you just know these numbers, you're probably able to fix it. It's not hard. But when I know all these numbers, it's just clear as day what's going on. So I gave you the tracking stuff. Use it. Now, end-of-day reports, some common mistakes with them. People just don't do them. Or they don't do them in Slack. You got to do them in Slack. If you just have them uploaded, the thing with Slack is that when the person submits the report, it creates a notification, then you want to clear it. If you just have your guys submitting these into Drive, then you're just never going to look at them. Just be honest with yourself. If I had my guys submitting theirs into Drive, I'd just never look at them. And if you don't submit it into Slack,

## [06:03 - 07:26]

the nice thing about Slack is then everybody can see the report. Rep's doing them wrong, so without detail, without effort. If the rep's doing it wrong, the tool doesn't work. If you don't coach them based on reports, so I've seen companies do them and then the people don't look at them or they don't coach off them, they're not keeping them accountable to do it every end of day. So every Monday through Friday, any day these guys work, there should be an end of day. So if you don't understand this, go through the end of day process training again if needed. Also, daily meetings come in pitfalls. Not doing them daily. Not training and drilling on almost every meeting. You could do the meetings, but if you're not training, drilling, if you're not adding value to these guys, you're going to be done. Not doing projections correctly. Like we had a bunch of trainings on projections, no accountability, treating projections like goals, no accuracy, so if you're not doing those correctly, not going to work. Poor energy on meetings. I've literally seen meetings where I could tell within 10 seconds, I was like, this seems sucks. So your energy needs to be good. No value provided on your meetings. If your sales reps are asking if they can skip the meetings and just take calls, your meetings suck. You don't suck, your meetings suck. So fix what you're doing on the meetings.
